source.title: Multiple factors based adaptive laboratory evolution strategy enhances DHA production in Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 by rewiring key metabolic pathways

initial_request.research_problem: Wild-type Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 has low DHA yield due to poor adaptation to low pH during fermentation.
initial_request.known_context: Wild-type strains isolated from natural environments typically fail to meet commercial demands for DHA yields, partly because they are poorly adapted to the decreased pH conditions encountered during fermentation.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Wild-type Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 fails to meet commercial DHA yields due to poor adaptation to low pH during fermentation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown whether multi-factor acclimation (low pH, low temperature, high DO) can synergistically enhance DHA production through complex metabolic rewiring.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multi-factor acclimation (low pH, low temperature, high DO) will synergistically enhance DHA production through complex metabolic rewiring.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated that multi-factor acclimation for DHA improvements would involve complex metabolic rewiring.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design orthogonal experimental ALE conditions to test hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: wild-type strains isolated from natural environments typically fail to meet commercial demands for DHA yields, partly because they are poorly adapted to the decreased pH conditions encountered during fermentation.
we hypothesize multi-factor acclimation for DHA improvements would involve complex metabolic rewiring that awaits dissection.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to implement ALE process to generate adapted strains.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Staged acidic ALE process with pH decreasing from 5.5 to 3.5 over 75 passages will yield acid-tolerant strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ALE process completed, yielding 12 endpoint strains.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize endpoint strains for biomass, TFA, and DHA.

evidence.quote_or_span: The entire ALE process involves 75 passages over a period of 150 days.

we used orthogonal experimental design to combine two levels of temperature (16 °C and 28 °C), two levels of DO indicated by the shaking speed (170 rpm and 230 rpm), and three acid types (citric acid, acetic acid, and hydrochloric acid) to obtain 12 combinations of ALE conditions

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to measure DCW, TFA, DHA under standard conditions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ALE strains will show altered biomass and DHA production compared to wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Culture wild-type and 12 ALE strains under standard conditions for 72 h.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Biomass, TFA, and DHA measured for all strains.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze effect of acid type on biomass.

evidence.quote_or_span: the wild-type strain and 12 ALE endpoint strains (Additional file 1: Table S1) were cultured under standard fermentation conditions (28 °C, 170 rpm, M4 medium) in triplicate. After 72 h of cultivation, these three indicators were measured.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Biomass data collected for all strains.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Determine which acid type yields highest biomass.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Citric acid acclimated strains will show higher biomass due to organic acid tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Citric acid acclimated strains showed higher biomass than other acids, indicating organic acid tolerance role.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate effect of additional stresses (low temperature, high DO) on biomass.

evidence.quote_or_span: Strains acclimated with citric acid generally showed higher biomass than those acclimated with other acids
This indicates that adaptability to specific acid types, rather than just a low pH value, contributes to improving biomass yield, suggesting a role of organic acid tolerance and/or remobilization

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Citric acid strains show highest biomass.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Determine if low temperature or high DO further increase biomass.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Low temperature or high DO alone will not further increase biomass beyond citric acid alone.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare biomass of citric acid strains with and without additional stresses.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Low temperature or high DO alone did not further increase biomass beyond citric acid alone.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Check TFA levels under these conditions.

evidence.quote_or_span: Compared with using citric acid alone as the acclimation condition, the addition of low-temperature or high-DO stress alone cannot further enhance the biomass accumulation ability.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Low temperature/high DO did not increase biomass.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Determine if these stresses affect TFA.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Low temperature and high DO increase TFA despite no biomass increase.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze TFA data for strains with additional stresses.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Low temperature and high DO increased TFA but not biomass, confirming they enhance fatty acid production via cellular physiology.

evidence.quote_or_span: applying low-temperature or high-DO stress alone significantly increased the TFA concentration in the ALE strain (Fig. 1A). This outcome starkly contrasts with the previous biomass results, further confirming that low-temperature and high-DO conditions enhance fatty acid production by maintaining normal cellular physiology and energy metabolism

evolution_trajectory.state_before: TFA increased by low temperature/high DO.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DHA/TFA fraction may decrease in most ALE strains.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate DHA/TFA fraction for all strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DHA/TFA fraction decreased in most ALE strains; only citric and acetic acid at normal conditions increased DHA fraction (42.3% wild-type, 47.0% citric, 51.6% acetic).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select best strain for transcriptomic analysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: the DHA/TFA fraction in most ALE strains decreased compared to that in the wild-type strain (42.3%), and increased in only two strains that were obtained through the citric acid (47.0%) and acetic acid (51.6%) acclimation at the normal temperature and oxygen level

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to understand transcriptional changes underlying improved DHA production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Transcriptomic analysis of ALE_AC_11 vs wild-type at 36 h and 48 h will reveal key metabolic rewiring.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform RNA-seq on ALE_AC_11 and wild-type at two time points.

evidence.quote_or_span: After 36 h of cultivation, a total of 23,118 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were identified

Through a comprehensive comparison of the transcriptome results of the two strains at two time points, we found that the transcriptionally active period of the wild-type strain did not end at 48 h of fermentation, and the difference in transcriptional activity was small compared with that at 36 h, which was mainly reflected in the small number of DEGs (Table 2).

These results indicate that the main mechanism for increasing the yield of TFA and DHA in the optimal ALE strain at 48 h of fermentation was the increase of acetyl-CoA, NADPH and PKS synthesis pathway fluxes.

Importantly, the ALE\_AC\_11 strain exhibited significantly lower expression of genes encoding RNA polymerase (RNAP) at 48 h than 36 h, indicating a potential decline in transcriptional activity during this metabolic transition period.

In addition to thraustochytrids, Crypthecodinium cohnii, produces high-purity DHA but has a lower biomass (20–30 g/L) and DHA yield (1.4–3 g/L), and is highly sensitive to dissolved oxygen (DO), limiting its industrial scale production $[10, 11]$ .

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Determine which pathways are enriched among DEGs.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DEGs will be enriched in carbohydrate, amino acid, lipid, and energy metabolism.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 1,113 genes in carbohydrate metabolism, 979 in amino acid metabolism, 707 in lipid metabolism, 618 in energy metabolism enriched.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate specific gene expression changes (GK, GPD).

evidence.quote_or_span: 1,113 genes were enriched in carbohydrate metabolism, 979 genes were enriched in amino acid metabolism, 707 genes were enriched in lipid metabolism, and 618 genes were enriched in energy metabolism
1,113 genes were enriched in carbohydrate metabolism, 979 genes were enriched in amino acid metabolism, 707 genes were enriched in lipid metabolism, and 618 genes were enriched in energy metabolism

, 36 h), a pathway enrichment analysis based on the annotations against the KEGG database showed that the gene expressions in the ALE\_AC\_11 strain compared to the wild-type strain were mainly enriched in two categories: metabolism and genetic information processing (Fig.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Pathway enrichment shows broad metabolic changes.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Determine if glycerol utilization genes are upregulated.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: GK and GPD upregulation suggests potential glycerol utilization even without glycerol in medium.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Examine expression of GK and GPD genes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Upregulation of GK and GPD in ALE_AC_11 despite no glycerol in medium suggests potential internal glycerol synthesis or metabolic readiness.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze temporal metabolic shift at 48 h.

evidence.quote_or_span: the up-regulated expression of glycerol kinase (GK) and glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GPD) coding genes suggest a potential of increase in the amount of glycerol-3-phosphate that enters the glycolytic pathway

Importantly, the ALE\_AC\_11 strain exhibited significantly lower expression of genes encoding RNA polymerase (RNAP) at 48 h than 36 h, indicating a potential decline in transcriptional activity during this metabolic transition period.

These results indicate that the main mechanism for increasing the yield of TFA and DHA in the optimal ALE strain at 48 h of fermentation was the increase of acetyl-CoA, NADPH and PKS synthesis pathway fluxes.

M4\_36 and M4\_48 represent wild-type cells incubated for 36 h and 48 h using M4 medium, respectively; M4C\_36 and M4C\_48 denote the acclimated strain ALE\_AC\_11 sampled after 36 h and 48 h of incubation

Through a comprehensive comparison of the transcriptome results of the two strains at two time points, we found that the transcriptionally active period of the wild-type strain did not end at 48 h of fermentation, and the difference in transcriptional activity was small compared with that at 36 h, which was mainly reflected in the small number of DEGs (Table 2).

In addition to thraustochytrids, Crypthecodinium cohnii, produces high-purity DHA but has a lower biomass (20–30 g/L) and DHA yield (1.4–3 g/L), and is highly sensitive to dissolved oxygen (DO), limiting its industrial scale production $[10, 11]$ .

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Transcriptomic analysis suggests improved DHA biosynthesis efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm improved DHA production in bioreactor.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ALE_AC_11 will show disproportionate DHA increase in bioreactor, indicating improved biosynthesis efficiency per unit biomass.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Cultivate ALE_AC_11 in 5-L bioreactor and measure DHA yield.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Disproportionate DHA increase suggests ALE improved DHA biosynthesis efficiency per unit biomass, not just biomass accumulation.

evidence.quote_or_span: the disproportionate rise in DHA yield suggests that ALE also improved DHA biosynthesis efficiency per unit biomass

In addition to thraustochytrids, Crypthecodinium cohnii, produces high-purity DHA but has a lower biomass (20–30 g/L) and DHA yield (1.4–3 g/L), and is highly sensitive to dissolved oxygen (DO), limiting its industrial scale production $[10, 11]$ .

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Bioreactor validation shows improved DHA efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Productivity gains smaller than flask cultures.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Reduced oxygen transfer and mixing limitations in bioreactor limit productivity gains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Bioreactor productivity gains were smaller than flask cultures, possibly due to reduced oxygen transfer and mixing limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: the differences in biomass and DHA productivity with the 5-L bioreactor cultures are much smaller than those observed with small-scale flask cultures. This can be attributed to a lower productivity gain in the ALE strain, potentially due to reduced oxygen transfer, limited resource mixing, and other scale effects in the bioreactor

metrics: DHA biosynthesis efficiency per unit biomass
success_verification.final_conclusion: Multi-factor ALE enhanced DHA production in Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 by rewiring key metabolic pathways, with improved DHA biosynthesis efficiency per unit biomass, though scale-up challenges remain.
limitations: Specific genetic mutations responsible for transcriptional changes remain undetermined.
Bioreactor productivity gains were smaller than flask cultures, possibly due to reduced oxygen transfer and mixing limitations.
